name: Gypsum Creek
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Monticello where gypsum crystals and selenite specimens are common in creek beds and exposed formations. Perfect for beginners seeking quality specimens without permits.
description:
Gypsum Creek offers accessible rockhounding on Utah BLM land in the scenic San Juan County region. The creek drainage exposes evaporite mineral deposits typical of the Colorado Plateau, where ancient inland seas left behind thick gypsum layers now weathered and fractured by modern erosion. Collectors regularly find clear selenite crystals, white gypsum specimens, and associated calcite deposits in the creek bed and along eroded hillsides. The site is beginner-friendly with no permit required and allows personal use collection up to 25 lbs per day. The relatively short walk from the parking area to productive collecting zones makes this an ideal destination for families and those new to rockhounding.

The geology here reflects the Permian and Triassic evaporite sequences that underlie much of the Colorado Plateau. As Gypsum Creek has cut through these deposits over millennia, it has created natural weathering that breaks apart large gypsum formations into portable specimens. You will find selenite (transparent gypsum variety) in clear, well-formed crystals, along with milky and opaque white gypsum masses. Associated minerals like calcite and quartz can appear in vugs and fractures.

Access is straightforward via BLM roads from Monticello, making this a practical half-day rockhounding trip. The terrain is semi-arid desert typical of southeastern Utah, with sparse pinyon-juniper vegetation and exposed red rock formations. Summer heat can be intense, so spring and fall visits are most comfortable. Water sources are limited to the creek itself, so bring adequate drinking water.

This location suits rockhounders of all experience levels. The abundant surface specimens require no special tools beyond a basic geology hammer and collection bag. Even casual visitors will spot quality gypsum crystals without extensive searching. More dedicated collectors can work the creek bed itself, where recent erosion constantly exposes fresh material.
history: Gypsum Creek drains through evaporite deposits laid down during the Permian period when Utah was covered by shallow inland seas. As these waters evaporated, they left behind thick beds of gypsum, halite, and other evaporite minerals. Modern stream erosion through these ancient deposits continues to expose and weather fresh specimens that surface rockhounders can readily collect.
tips: Work the active creek bed and the eroded banks immediately adjacent to the water - this is where weathering has broken apart the largest intact specimens. Look for transparent selenite crystals in clusters on the surface and within loose debris; they often reflect sunlight and stand out against red soil. Bring a 1-2 lb geology hammer to carefully extract specimens from partially exposed formations without damaging crystal faces. The most productive areas are usually within the first quarter-mile upstream from the main parking pullout, where creek-side erosion has been most active.
terrain_notes: Semi-arid desert terrain at roughly 5,400 feet elevation. Sparse pinyon-juniper woodland with exposed red rock formations and sandy-gravelly soil. The creek drainage is relatively gentle with easy walking along the streambed. No sustained elevation gain on the main collecting routes. Footing is uneven with scattered loose rocks and cobbles in the creek bed, but hiking is straightforward for most fitness levels. Vegetation is minimal, providing excellent visibility of mineral specimens on the ground.
best_season: October - April. Fall and spring offer comfortable temperatures and lower UV exposure. Summer (May-September) brings intense desert heat regularly exceeding 90 degrees F, making midday collecting exhausting and hazardous. Winter (December-February) is possible but occasional snow can briefly block access roads.
written_directions: From Monticello, take US 191 south for approximately 8 miles. Watch for BLM marker signs indicating access roads on the east side near the San Juan River drainage. Turn east onto marked BLM Road 509 and follow it approximately 3 miles on a well-maintained dirt road suitable for passenger vehicles. Look for the Gypsum Creek drainage pullout marked by a weathered BLM sign. Park in the designated area and walk northeast into the creek drainage - the main collecting zone is within a 5 to 10-minute walk from the parking area along the streambed.
road_conditions: Well-maintained dirt BLM road, washboard texture common in dry season. Passenger vehicle clearance adequate. No high-clearance vehicle required under normal conditions. Road may be impassable after heavy rain or snowmelt.
parking_notes: Unimproved pullout area with capacity for 3-5 vehicles. No facilities. Parking is informal - pull off the main road into the designated turnaround area. No day-use fee or reservation required.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Collectors must not damage formations beyond specimen removal. Pack out all trash. Leave the site in the condition you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
Do not damage formations
Pack out all trash
Leave site undisturbed for other users
nearest_services: Monticello, approximately 8 miles west. Offers gas, groceries, and dining. No services at the rockhounding site itself.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Uneven creek bed with scattered loose rocks requires sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to the productive collecting areas. Short walk from parking (5-10 minutes) minimizes strain. The main hazard is uneven footing near the creek; those with limited mobility should stay in the immediate parking area where surface specimens are also common.

faq.question: What minerals can I find rockhounding at Gypsum Creek near Monticello?
faq.answer: Gypsum Creek is known for selenite crystals, white gypsum specimens, calcite deposits, and quartz. Selenite is the transparent variety of gypsum and often forms beautiful clear crystals that are highly prized by collectors. Most specimens are found loose on the surface or partially embedded in the creek bed, making collection straightforward for beginners.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Gypsum Creek, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required to collect at Gypsum Creek - it is open BLM land managed under personal use rockhounding guidelines. You can collect free of charge up to 25 lbs per day with no advance registration or forms. This makes it an excellent low-cost rockhounding destination for families and casual collectors.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Gypsum Creek for rockhounding?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal, as desert temperatures in southeastern Utah are comfortable during these months. Summer heat frequently exceeds 90 degrees F and makes daytime rockhounding exhausting. Spring (March-April) and fall (September-October) offer the most pleasant conditions with lower UV exposure and stable weather.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Gypsum Creek, and is it suitable for families with children?
faq.answer: Gypsum Creek is rated easy difficulty with a short walk from parking and relatively flat, open terrain. It is highly suitable for families with children ages 6 and up. Surface specimens are abundant and require minimal effort to locate, making it ideal for introducing beginners and kids to rockhounding without frustration.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to rockhound at Gypsum Creek?
faq.answer: A small geology hammer (1-2 lbs), collection bag, sturdy hiking boots, and plenty of water are the essentials. The abundant surface specimens can be collected by hand or with light tapping to remove from the creek bed. Wear sunscreen and a hat for UV protection, as there is minimal shade in the semi-arid landscape. No specialized power tools or heavy equipment is needed or permitted.

faq.question: How far is Gypsum Creek from Monticello, and what are the driving directions?
faq.answer: Gypsum Creek is approximately 8 miles south of Monticello via US 191. Turn east onto marked BLM Road 509 and follow it about 3 miles on a dirt road suitable for standard passenger vehicles. The drive takes approximately 20-30 minutes total from Monticello, making it an accessible half-day rockhounding trip from town.

meta_description: Find selenite crystals and gypsum specimens at Gypsum Creek near Monticello, Utah. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit required. Beginner-friendly.
